exemplar.title: How does increasing soil salinity of 0M, 0.2M, 0.5M, 0.8M, and 1M (NaCl) affect the germination rate of and initial growth of pisum sativum (garden peas) within a two-week period?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does increasing soil salinity of 0M, 0.2M, 0.5M, 0.8M, and 1M (NaCl) affect the germination rate of and initial growth of pisum sativum (garden peas) within a two-week period?

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Report follows IA structure with clear headings, tables of contents and consistent navigation
Use of appropriate scientific terminology (osmotic stress, ion toxicity, molarity) is consistent
weaknesses: Verbosity, duplicated paragraphs and run-on sentences reduce concision
Inconsistencies (3 vs. 5 trials, cotton vs. soil) interrupt logical flow
Grammar slips and formatting inconsistencies (citation style, unit notation) impede clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure and organization are strong enough for band-3 in that strand, but weaknesses in concision and occasional logical breaks confine the overall communication performance to band 2. The work is generally clear but requires tighter phrasing, consistent formatting and correction of inconsistencies to achieve higher marks.

strengths: Clear, coherent and focused research question with precisely defined IV, DV and timeframe
Rich contextualisation of soil salinisation using relevant statistics and geographic focus
Comprehensive explanation of links between salinisation, food security and the germination study
weaknesses: Minor redundancy in wording (phrase âgermination rate and early growthâ repeated)
Some citations in the environmental context are vague
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands of the context criterion meet the 5-6 band descriptors. The research question is coherent and drives data collection unambiguously; the environmental issue is discussed with relevant statistics and context; connections are explained without significant omissions. The only barriers to perfection are minor redundancies and citation vagueness, which do not undermine the overall excellence, justifying a top-band score of 6.

strengths: Detailed, repeatable method that gathers sufficient data across five salinity levels and multiple trials
Clear description of sampling strategy with consistent seed counts and replicates
Identification of key hazards and generic ethical considerations
weaknesses: Terminology inconsistency between âcottonâ and âsoilâ substrate
Sampling replicates are described inconsistently (3 vs. 5 trials) without statistical justification
Risk assessment lacks likelihood/severity ranking and detailed mitigation measures
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The method design strand firmly sits in the 5-6 band, being appropriate and repeatable. The sampling strategy and risk strands fall into the 3-4 and 1-2 bands respectively, dragging the holistic performance down by one band. Overall this warrants a strong band-5 score, reflecting very good planning with only minor clarity and depth omissions.

strengths: Meaningful suggestions for increasing sample size, salinity increments and measurement time points
Identification of several method strengths and limitations (controlled environment, sample size concerns)
Discussion links findings back to the environmental issue of salinisation and food security
weaknesses: Evaluation of the conclusion is descriptive with omissions of alternative explanations
Method evaluation lacks depth on magnitude of limitations and measurement error
Improvement suggestions are relevant but lack detailed implementation plans
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three DEV strands fall into the 3-4 band: the evaluation is partial, the method discussion is descriptive, and improvements, while relevant, are not fully developed. The performance is clearly in the middle of the band, and a score of 4 reflects meaningful evaluation and extension suggestions without comprehensive depth.

strengths: Appropriate construction of raw data tables and two clear graphs showing seedling length trends
Correct calculation of means and identification of the overall inverse growth trend
Conclusion is valid and fully supported by the presented numerical data
weaknesses: Germination-rate data promised by the research question are omitted from presentation
No measures of variability or statistical analysis (e.g., SD, error bars)
Processed data tables left some blank average cells
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data presentation and analysis are solid but incomplete, with key omissions of germination rates and dispersion measures, placing both in the 3-4 band. The conclusion is well-supported by data, meriting the upper end of that band. A holistic score of 4 accurately reflects correct but partial data treatment and interpretation.

strengths: Well-justified proposal of multiple applications (salt-tolerant crops, irrigation techniques, policy measures) based directly on findings
Clear linkage between experimental results and the rationale for each solution
weaknesses: Evaluation of solution strengths and weaknesses is unbalanced, omitting cost, feasibility barriers and implementation details
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The application proposal strand solidly meets the top band with a well-justified solution based on results. However, the solution evaluation remains in the 1-2 band due to lack of balanced appraisal and implementation detail. On balance, this criterion merits the maximum 3 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: Scientific terms (osmotic stress, ion toxicity, molarity) are used consistently, yet verbosity, grammatical slips and repeated phrases reduce concision. Hence the work meets either appropriate terminology OR concision, not both.

The student links findings to salinisation impacts and food security, but evaluation of how well the conclusion resolves the issue is brief and omits alternative explanations or broader limitations. This is a partial evaluation with omissions.

The conclusion interprets the decreasing trend and relates magnitudes to the hypothesis with numerical reference (12.82 cm to 3.08 cm). Although lacking statistics, the trend interpretation is valid and fully supported by the data presented, meriting the upper end of the 3-4 band.

Means are calculated correctly; qualitative patterns are identified, but no statistical tests, variability measures or rate calculations are provided. Analysis is correct where done but incomplete, fitting the 3-4 band.

Suggestions include more salinity increments, larger sample size, multi-time measurements and control of other abiotic factorsâmeaningful and linked to identified weaknesses, yet not detailed enough to be âsignificant modificationsâ across all major weaknesses.

Step-wise method, variable table and apparatus list allow replication and gather sufficient data (5 concentrations Ã 5 trials Ã 10 seeds). Measurements (mm, %) and time frame are declared. Minor inconsistencies (cotton called âsoilâ, conflicting seed counts) slightly weaken clarity, but overall the design is appropriate and repeatable.

Connections between salinisation, food security and the chosen germination study are explained: garden peasâ nitrogen fixation, economic value and salt sensitivity are linked to the Basinâs challenges and wider regions. The narrative is logically developed with few omissions, meeting the descriptor for a comprehensive explanation.

The student states a clear, focused and relevant question including identifiable IV levels, DVs and time frame. The wording is precise enough to drive data collection without ambiguity, showing coherence with later sections; only a minor redundancy in wording (-âgermination rate and early growthâ appears twice) prevents a perfect 6.

Strengths (yield, resilience) and weaknesses (costs not discussed, feasibility vague) are only partially evaluated. The student lists benefits without a balanced, detailed appraisal, fitting the middle band.

The IA follows the prescribed section order with TOC, headings, tables and figures, maintaining clear navigation throughout. Occasional duplication does not disrupt overall organisation, matching a well-structured descriptor.

Strengths and limitations are listed (controlled environment, sample size, single-time measurement) but not fully discussed in depth (no assessment of magnitude, no comment on measurement error). The coverage is descriptive rather than a full discussion.

The environmental issue of soil salinisation is discussed with statistics (20 % irrigated land; 12 % Kenyan population food-insecure) and geographical focus (Tana River Basin). Causes, impacts and global parallels are outlined, providing rich context. Some citations are vague, so depth is slightly short of âcomprehensiveâ excellence.

The student describes using 10 seeds per dish and repeats trials to improve reliability, but numbers vary (3 trials mentioned earlier, 5 later) and no justification is given for choosing 10 seeds or five repeats based on statistical power. Thus the strategy is described rather than fully justified.

Key hazards (NaCl irritation, glass cuts, spills) and ethical disposal are outlined, but the assessment lacks likelihood/severity ranking or mitigation detail beyond generic PPE; ethical aspects regarding seed use are minimal. This constitutes an outline rather than a comprehensive description.

Raw tables and two graphs present seedling length for all concentrations, yet germination-rate data promised in the RQ are absent and error bars/SDs are omitted. Therefore diagrams are appropriate but have omissions.

A range of applications (salt-tolerant crops, drip irrigation, drainage, IPM, policy) is proposed with rationale referencing research findings. The justification shows how outcomes inform each solution, satisfying the highest band for this strand.

Arguments generally flow, but inconsistencies (3 vs 5 trials, cotton vs soil, duplicated paragraphs) interrupt clarity. The narrative is mainly logical but occasionally hard to follow, aligning with the 2-mark descriptor.

annotations.comment: Solution evaluation lists benefits but omits detailed drawbacks or implementation barriers. Provide a balanced appraisal of strengths and weaknesses.
References include web links but lack consistent formatting (e.g., italics for journal names). Use a single citation style (APA or MLA) uniformly.
The overall structure follows IA guidelines, but some headings appear mid-paragraph. Ensure each major section begins on a new line for readability.
Several minor grammar slips and run-on sentences reduce clarity. Perform a concise proofreading pass to tighten language and correct slips.
The research question is clear and focused, defining IV concentrations, DVs and timeframe well. Remove the duplicated phrase âgermination rate and early growthâ to improve concision.
The table of contents is well structured, aiding navigation. Consider streamlining labels to match section headings exactly for consistency.
Environmental issue section effectively cites statistics, but the same content appears on pages 4 and 5. Remove duplicate paragraphs for clarity.
Hypothesis justification references osmotic stress and ion toxicity appropriately. Strengthen by including quantitative thresholds from prior studies to link directly to chosen concentrations.
The variable table labels âCottonâ but earlier called it âsoil.â Ensure consistent terminology when describing controlled substrate.
Sampling strategy explains 10 seeds per dish and three versus five trials inconsistently. Clarify the exact number of replicates and justify based on statistical reliability.
Risk assessment lists hazards but omits likelihood and severity rankings. Include a risk matrix and more mitigation details (e.g., eyewash station location).
The contextual introduction outlines why Pisum sativum was chosen, but the paragraph repeats details. Merge overlapping sentences on control selection to avoid redundancy.
Conclusion aptly describes inverse growth trend but lacks statistical support. Reference quantitative tests or significance levels to robustly support claims.
Method step specifies â0.2%â salinity when earlier the variable table used molarity. Correct units to â0.2 Mâ to avoid confusion.
Data collection steps include two identical items (#3 and #4). Remove duplication and clarify unique steps.
Raw data tables for 0 M concentrations lack the calculated averages row. Populate the blank cells to complete the data presentation.
Processed data tables omit measures of dispersion (e.g., SD, error bars). Incorporate variability indicators to strengthen data analysis.
The processed summary graph shows seedling length but neglects germination rate although in the research question. Present germination data visually as well.
The discussion links results to the environmental issue but remains descriptive. Deepen evaluation by comparing to alternative explanations or regional variability.
Method evaluation lists strengths and limitations but lacks depth in assessing measurement error and environmental controls. Discuss magnitude of limitations.
Improvement suggestions are relevant but general. Propose a specific plan (e.g., sample-size calculation, monitoring intervals at days 3, 7, 14) to be more meaningful.
Application proposals cover solutions broadly. To evaluate feasibility, include cost-benefit considerations or case examples for each measure.
Consistency in numeric notation varies (e.g., mm, cm, %). Standardize units throughout to avoid confusion.
